speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The real man and he was a great swimmer and he anybody that could swim really was ace high with him. Well another kid and I we swam across the river one fall or it's about a mile and a half two miles across and we swam in all damn near Icewater and I think that's why I got that recommendation through him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah yeah I put him one summer with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Labland?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that what was that like?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well we hired the backpackers to take care of our equipment you know and of course there's a lot of long lakes up in there and we use boats along the lakes and in some places we had to row. One time we were at the horse for 24 hours. My God I'll tell you it took me 14 days to get over that. I was tired for 14 days I'll tell you. Well I'll tell you how it was. We went up this lake and then there's a quite cataracts between it and the next lake so there was a portage and there was a boat at the other end that we could use and there was a settler there and he said and there by the way they what you call it now what you call it here there the guy that the minister that takes care of the internal affairs of the country.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh that's the Secretary of the Interior.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah the Secretary of Interior about a week before that him and him and his wife and a daughter tried to make it and they got stuck. The wind. All I had was a rowboat you know and I'll tell you when the wind comes down along in those narrow lakes she really turns up now. So the settler said well he says I'll tell you it's been blowing up for a week and it ain't gonna last very long. If I was you I'd start out now and the captain he took his word for it we started out and I'll tell you if we hadn't started then we'd never got up. Because after we got through Mason the streams that we had to mace her up at the upper end of this lake she was blowing down the lake oh man it was blowing and because I was the boatman generally and we just rode a little ways and then I rode a show and I cut a birch you know those boats they're always like so and there's always a piece or bent wood up in front where you can grab a holder if you want to beach them. Well I stuck the birch in between the bow and that little piece that you and I'll tell you that's the best thing you could think of. We just went down that lake of Helen we never had to row a stroke. No kidding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the birch did it for you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That birch stood just like that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I called him the captain. See this fellow that was ahead for the party he had put in he graduated from the Swedish luck you'd call West Point you know and then he went to France and hired out with a foreign legion in Congo you know and he got pensioned off of there then he went back to Sweden and got a job on the hydrographic bureau so I always called him captain. Well listen I want to ask you why you decided to leave Sweden and come to Elk River. Well I tell you all my friends left. See I was raised in a little county seat about 12 13 miles from where I went to high school and my dad had a hardware store there and all my friends left. I got them scattered all over the west coast here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part of Sweden were you from?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Northern part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part? von Land?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No no no no no way up north. For the fact the town where I went to high school that's where the ship out all the iron ore from.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah I've seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's what do they call that province or that part of Sweden that you were from?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: North button.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was the county seat?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Gammelstad. That means old town. You see that that land comes up at the rate of about three feet every hundred years. The land races up out of the Baltic and that was the original town but they finally on account of some finally the land raised so much that they had trouble getting up there with sailboats and so they moved the town out about 12 miles further towards the Baltic and that's where she is now. That town will stay where it is but they're gonna have to move the harbor.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Of course they got a narrow path that's one of the finest harbors you could think of. The river just widens out and makes a big lake but there's a little narrow passage to get into this lake and they've been dredging that passage for 70 years. They just keeping ahead of the racing of the land. They claim the land. I talked to the geologist when I was home. He was staying in the same hotel where I was and he said it's on account of the Ice Age. The ice pressed it down and now she's shaping up again. See like Finland now. That was all under an Arctic Ocean at one time. There's a little, what do you call it, bivalve like oysters and stuff. There's a little bitty one that's scattered all through the soil of Finland. The Latin name for it is Yoldia artica. So they know that at one time Finland was under an Arctic sea because that's the only place where this little thing will live is in the Arctic waters.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well listen, you say that most of your friends left and came to the United States.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now I'm wondering why they left and why did they stay put in Sweden?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No work. That's before Sweden industrialized. They know Swedes immigrate anymore. They're even important labor at times since they're industrialized. You see, I tell you, Germany and Holland and France and England and Spain and Italy and them, they all had colonies, you know, and they kept the trade tight. You stay out of my colonies, see, and they couldn't sell hardly to any of those. Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Switzerland and oh a whole lot of little countries like that that didn't have any colonies. They were just left without.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But now, since those colonies have broke away from the mother country and they even had China, you know, each one had their, did you call it kong clay? Ain't that what they called it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Something like that. Yeah, yeah, they had it all divided and they kept other people, other nations out. So a lot of them little countries, they were just there was nothing to do.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: One fellow whose parents came from Sweden told me that his parents came to get away from militarism.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah, oh yes, oh yes, oh yeah, see there's compulsory military training. And, but that, I'll tell you, a lot of them stayed and took that. But that's really what gives you a kick in the ass to go. But it isn't really that, that, that, that, it isn't really the reason that they go. The reason they go is there's no word. There was no word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it kind of futile?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I mean, he's, he indicated to me that, that a few rich people owned most of the land and most of the people didn't have anything. I guess down in the very southern part, there's quite a few landowners. I mean then, when, when you came over. No, not up north where I were. No, no, there, there, we have the most independent farmers you ever saw. And they all owned their land. Oh yeah, no, no. But in southern Sweden, I guess they still have some big estates that, and then they have people that work it for them.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But not, not up north.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't there a big strike in about 1910 in Sweden?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. I read about that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember, you remember that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah, I was in high school then. Hell, I'll tell you, there wasn't a goddamn soul worked. Nobody worked. They really had a strike. Now they tied up the whole country. Yeah. Oh yes, I remember that. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: How, how do you think it was so, it happened that they did so well, that it was so effective, the strike, you know, that, that they shut everything down. You could never imagine doing that in a, in a place like the United States, but in Sweden they shut everything down.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah, they shut everything down. Well,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: you couldn't make them work. No, they shut her down tight, I tell you. They really shut her down. Oh. Well, the labor unions were just getting strong then. They were, they were just starting. And, and of course, up in the Baltic, along the coast there on them islands and stuff, there's old log cabins all over where fishermen used to live, and then the fishermen teetered out and those log cabins just stand there empty. Oh, I'll tell you, I guess every one of them was pulled that stuff. There's people that were on strike, you know, and had a sailboat, you know, they'd go out there and fish and lay around. Oh, I guess they had a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, yeah, I can remember that. I, I, I was in high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, look, uh, what about 1910?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's when I think it was. I think you're just about right. You're just about right. Yeah. Yeah, I think it was 1910.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you decided to come to Elk River though because, because you knew somebody that was already here?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Eric Soderberg is his name. He's retired and lives in, uh, Reed's port. It's all in Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had known him well in, uh, from your hometown?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, we went to, we went to grade school together. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say to convince you that it was worth coming all this way?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I read a lot of stories about the good hunting and fishing and stuff, you know, and I always had itchy feet.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And, uh,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: there was another kid. He's retired and lived in Seattle. He, he ended up as, training me a foreman for the Elliott Day Lumber Company, Ruben. His name is Ruben Runnier. We came over together. We traveled together, Ruben and I. And, well, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it easy to, uh, get the papers to come to America at that time?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah, yeah. If you hadn't signed up for the army, you know, if you do it before you register for, uh, compulsory military training, you can go. You're free. But once you register, then you're, they got you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's why it's a kick, a kick to come, to do it before you have to sign up for the army?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's right. That's right. That's why a lot of them, uh, they hate to spend all that time, uh, with the army. It's all right. It ain't bad, but they hate to lose that time, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much time was it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, of course, it's, it's not all in one hitch, but it's, it's close to two years altogether, you know. And, uh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I got a thing that's been hit, John. You can, you can keep it on now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you got it on?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I always laugh about that. Has George, the name, Jordan Valley, come into your, very much, you heard of him. Of course, there were two Jordan Valley's. For Jordan Valley, my chum, he was the father of the Jordan Valley, that, uh, deeper agent in, in Bovill. And George was telling me one time about when, uh, George and a couple of the other guys got drunk, and one of them got pinched and sold in jail. Well, the other fellas felt sorry for him. So they went and smashed one of the windows in the back of the jail, and they, uh, flattened the stovepipe and stuck it in there. Then they'd pour whiskey up at the upper end of the stovepipe, and a guy had guzzled it at the other end. They called his stovepipe cocktails. And when the sheriff came to let him out the next morning, he was drunkard, and when he put him in the night before.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I always used to get a cheek out of that stove.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But you, you can, you can cut tape. Oh, sure. Oh, sure. Yeah. You know, one time, I'll tell you a good one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know where that maintenance pile is along the highway about three miles out of Elk River? Towards Bovill?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Yeah. Well, there used to be a logging spur go up and up along those meadows, those last meadows that you go through before you go over the Hump in 12th River. They had a logging spur there, and there was two sporting houses up at the end of those meadows, and the logging spur went right by these houses. So there was Coyote, Smith, and Sony Nelson. They got drunk one night, and here was the Milwaukee Indians tied up for the night by the depot. So they gets on the engine and went down, around the pond, and down to where they got to that place where that maintenance pile is now, and then they switched in on the track and went over to the sporting house. And everything went all right, and they went back. They got out on the main line and threw the switch and went back into town, and when they came in front of the depot, they went through an open switch and put it on the ground. The next morning, you couldn't find, you couldn't find Coyote Smith or Sony Nelson in town. But Coyote Smith had a friend, Holmes, that are Scott. He had a home that's right about Bob Haynes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the lower basin is on Elk Creek?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, more or less, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, Bob Haynes was down by the lower basin, and Scott was right up above. So that's where the guys took off for Scott's homestead. And Bloom, the General Manager, the old Swede General Manager, he was looking for him. But then Elmet Torgensen, he was running the pool hall down on the front street down there by the depot. And the homesteaders had sent word into Elmer what Coyote Smith and Sony Nelson needed. And he'd get it and get butter and eggs and coffee and sugar, whatever needed. And he'd fill this pack sack and set it back to the pool hall in the evening. And the next morning it would be gone. That's how they put in the winter. Well, I guess in the spring, Bloom had kind of got over most of it. And they went back and went to work finally. They worked for years for the Potlatch after that. But that's how they went to the It's a good story.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's a real good story. I had a laugh one time.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Elmet Torgensen, he had a shed in the back. Instead of having wood shed with walls, he just had four big log posts and then a roof. And it was all open there. And he had a restaurant in the pool hall, Elmet Torgensen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know them old fashioned fly traps that they used to have? How did this one work?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, the flies got in there someplace and they tried to get up. It was screaming. And every morning, the cook used to, every evening, the cook used to take them out and pour hot water over them. So he'd kill the flies and then he'd shake off the dead flies and put back sugar water and stuff in them and set them inside. And that's the only way they had to catch flies those days, you know. Well, Charlie Johnson and Jordan Valley was sitting on the chopping block killing a court. And the cook had hung up one of these fly traps on a nail on one of these posts, but he hadn't killed the flies yet. And they sat there and they got drunker and they got drunker. And finally, Charlie Johnson looked at those flies busting up there and he grabbed an axe and he said, Beattie, Beattie!

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And he chopped Elmer was mad for a while, but he got over it. He had another post pudding. Beattie, Beattie!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you beat that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Have you ever heard anything about the...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: A Glenn Wagner and... That fellow's name used to run the pool hall down by the depot. Adams! Adams! Yeah, I've heard his name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see now, what was your first name?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I've heard his name, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, anyway, what the hell was your first name?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: It doesn't matter. I can see. I can check his name.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Well, one time, and I was in the days of traveling salesman, you know, Harry Adams. I already named Harry. And old Harry was an awful liar. Oh, God. Well, here was this traveling salesman on hotter than the dickens in the middle of the summer. And here he came with a big suitcase, and he just missed the train. And he came in the pool hall, and he was a cousin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, well, Harry, where do you want to go? Where do you want to go and get to Lewiston?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, Harry said, you can catch the steamboat to Lewiston. And so he asked him where the steamboat left for Lewiston. Well, he said, just go down there towards the coal dock down the pond. That's where the steamboat goes to Lewiston. George Elainey, another friend of mine, was a pawn boss. And here come this guy dragging a big suitcase and sweating like hell, looking for the steamboat for Lewiston.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: George Elainey said, he just died here about a year ago. He said, oh, my God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, who told you that there was a steamboat for Lewiston?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, that man up in the pool, oh, oh, oh, no wonder. So here this guy came back in the pool hall and started to cuss Harry. And Harry didn't know what he was talking about. Why, man, I never told you that. You must be crazy with the heat. That's as far as he got.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There used to be a family across the street where Harry ate lunch. And Harry was bald -headed as hell, you know. His head was just like a billiard ball. And this family wasn't. I guess old man Bell had all kinds of reason not to trust his wife too far. But Harry never hung around there at all, you know. Old man Bell was an old fool, anyway. And Harry was sitting up at the counter eating his lunch. And old man Bell was putting some wood in the stove in the middle of the pool hall and a restaurant. He went over and felt Harry's bald head. You know, he said that heat just like my wife's ass. And Harry, he, by God, he does. Yes, sir. He does just exactly like it. Yes, sir. And you know, man Bell got so mad he wanted to fight.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I don't know. It sounds like he was asking for it. He was asking for it. But the best one, though, was there was a fellow by...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bob Muir?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. And I guess he was quite a duck hunter in Montana. It was in the fall. And Charlie McNough, a friend of mine, we were partners loading cars those days. And he was just as honest as the days long. Charlie never told a lie in his life. Well.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Harry had already... Bob Muir asked him...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want a cigarette?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I like a muscle. This had already happened before Charlie got in the pool hall. Harry had told Bob Muir, he hung around there for a week to see how the trade was, see. And he wanted to know about ducks. Well, Harry said, there's a tree mile long lake above the lumber yard and he's just black with ducks. So Bob Muir had went and borrowed a shotgun and bought a box of shells. And then he came back down the pool hall. And that's when Charlie McNough, this friend of mine, was sitting by the stove. And of course, Coyote Smith. He's one of the guys that went to the sporting house with the engine. He came in and he was just as bad as Harry.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And here in came Bob Muir and Coyote Smith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Harry said, say, Coyote, that lake up above the lumber yard, are there very many ducks up there?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Out of the way, Coyote Sparred. Ducks. Why, that lake is just black with ducks. From one... For three miles there is nothing but ducks. And oh, they talked about the ducks up in that lake and here said Charlie by the stove. Taking it all in and finally he couldn't stand it no longer. He said, by God, I've lived here for 20 years and there ain't no lake up there.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He spoiled it all.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And always, after much, when Charlie McNough and kid Bob Muir about the lake above the lumber yard, he talked a little through his nose, Bob Muir did. Final science, last time he did.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, I'll tell you that, Harry. About every three months, there was a sawyer there, Dewey Lavoy, was his name, little fella, really short fella. He was a sawyer in the sawmill.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And when he'd always come into the pool hall and buy a chocolate bar before he went to work and he'd eat it, what he was sawing in the afternoon, Harry would get him back on the counter and hey, he said, I'll tell you, the overhead was up this afternoon, this afternoon. They're shuttered on a down Saturday. He's gonna, but don't tell anybody, don't tell anybody that he's supposed to be a secret. She's gonna be shut down Saturday. When the men came by the pool hall from work in the evening after the whistle blew, a dozen guys came in and told Harry that the sawmill were shutting down Saturday.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And he pulled that on Dewey all the time.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You think they'd learn never to trust him anything he said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, I do.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: A lot of stories I could tell. They ain't fit for things.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I don't know about that. So far, they've all been awful good for, you know, we just keep the tapes. We could always soften it down or change the names if we ever used it, you know, but they're all good stories.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But one good story, Bill Morris, the druggist, was a very good friend of mine. And he had been eating beans or something. And he just got him a new Willis Knight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Willis Knight car?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. And he was going down to Lewiston, him and his wife. She's still living up at Earth River. And

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: they were coming down that Henry grade and Bill couldn't hold it any longer. So he'd sneak one. And he always waited till the car, till I met a car, and he'd sneak one. And he said, hey, Bill, I can smell something. Oh, yeah. It's that car that we just met. Oh, he waited and put his shoulder till he either met or passed another car, and then he'd sneak another one. And yeah, it's that car we passed. And when he passed the third car, yeah, Mabel said, I can smell that now. You know I never noticed that before.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Now, he'd had a pretty good one.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I'll tell you, Mabel, she never could take a joke. And she was just about a straight laced and good woman as there ever was, you know. And Bill had been out hunting on Medaridge with the boys. And a couple, three of them decided to go home. So he came home in the middle of the night and came and looked, knocked on the bedroom window.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Mabel said, is that you, Will?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was you expecting?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: God damn it, she didn't speak to him for two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, we used to, we used to, I don't know, have you ever run across the name Albert Wiley?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I don't think so, not yet, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's he?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He's, his father had a homestead at the bottom of the falls right here, below the, right, right where Deep Creek and Elm Creek come together. The homestead was right there in that point. I'd always get a kick out of Albert. Miss Merman was the school teacher out in that little schoolhouse. They used to call it the Startered Schoolhouse. It was just up from the falls little ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, one time Miss Merman asked Albert what was the most important sun or the moon?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I guess Albert had walked home from Elk River in the moonlight so many times that he really appreciated the moon. So he told her that the moon was the most important. Well, Miss Merman asked him what he based his opinion on. Well, the moon shines at night when it's dark and the sun shines in the daytime when it's daylight then away.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The funny thing, the old man, he talked all right, but all the, not all the kids, but Albert especially, he was talked awful slow. And they were hauling wood into town. The old man was selling wood and Albert was helping him. It was in the wintertime and snow, you know, that pulled up the alley, this alley and right in front of the hole where you throw it in to whoever was going to buy it. And Albert said, go a little further, Dad. No, it's all right here. It's all right. Yeah, I wish you'd go a little further. No, it's all right here. We can sort of, yeah, I know it's all right, but you stopped the sled runner on my foot.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I got, you know, during the depression, I was working for the road crew and we tried to get all the fellows that wanted to join the CCC. And Albert was staying in an old cabin on the Orofino road, that dent road, you know. And the fellow that was in charge, he wanted me to see Albert. So I was running road truck car, all I had was oil and gas and stuff. And I just tagged a long time, you know. So when we came to to roll us cabin, I stopped and I walked down and there was Albert. I tried to talk him into, he was sleeping on some bows in a corner. There was no doors or windows in the cabin. And he didn't have any socks. All he had was shoes. I don't think he had any underwear. I told him he'd get three meals a day and he'd get paid and he'd get some clothes and stuff. And I tried to sell the CCC to him. Albert says, well, he says, I tell you, George, I got quite a little stuff to eat here and I got a good bed and as far as clothes is concerned, I got just about all I care to pack around right now. So he didn't join the CCC. But he finally joined later on in the season. And at the end of the season, he came up to Elk River and Bill Morris and I always kind of kept to me under our wing, you know. These folks were all gone. And after he'd been with the CCCs a while, he joined the blister rust. And Bill asked him about the CCCs, the blister rust. Oh, that was all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how did you like the CCCs?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, that was all right too. I would have had a lot of money too if the other CCC boys hadn't borrowed it from me. So that's all he thought of money. Yeah. That sounds like he was a kind of simple -minded guy. He was a little and still he could take a little flour and sugar and a 30 -30 carbine and go out and live in the woods for weeks and months. So he wasn't so simple either in a lot of ways. You know, a lot of smart people couldn't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Oh, Albert could take a little flour and salt and a 30 -30 carbine and he'd make it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he do that? Live in the woods?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah, he did that lots of times. He's in a nursing home in Bend, Oregon now.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He said, he said, he telling about we went out to look at some traps and we had a bobcat in one of them. So we killed him and I stuck him in my pack sack and we couldn't shoot him. Of course, my brother Levi had shot all the shells away at the ground squirrels. But after I had him in my pack sack a while, he started to bite and scratch. So we had to take him out and kill him again.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I used to get a lot of kikata Albert. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a friendly man? Was he really friendly to you, to other people? Oh, Albert?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Oh, very friendly. Oh, yes, he was a nice boy that way. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many homesteaders around in the country at the time when you came?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, there was especially south. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Especially south and east of Elk River. All the land was taken up the home. There was a log cabin on putting it every quarter section. But they're all gone now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there an idea to prove up and then sell the Potlatch?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I think a lot. There was an awful hunger for land about that time. About the early part of the century. And they actually tried to make a go of it, a lot of them. But then prices kept on going up. And then the First World War come along, and they got jobs in town and prices went sky high even then, you know, compared with the stuff that you had to buy. And so that finished the homesteaders. A lot of them tried to cut a few ties and haul to town and sell cut wood, you know, and they'd go out and work and live on the homestead. But their original idea was to make a home out there. A lot of them. But the First World War seeks that. They just had to go out and go to work in order to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have anything on Summerfield when they caught the bootleggers in the park country?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I've heard about Summerfield, but not about the park country, no. Not about the park country, but not about Summerfield out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah, he went out there. Well, I know he went all around the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the park country is, don't you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, he went out there, you know, and all those moonshiners out there, they whined him and dined him, you know. And he was going to sell the moonshine for him. And he told him, he said, I'll have to be awful careful so that goddamn Summerfield don't catch up with me. He told him. And oh, boy. And then after he got them all rounded up, he just went to town and got him a bunch of deputies. They just rounded up the whole work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Haven't you ever heard that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. In fact, I know one guy that got caught in it. Name is Earl Hammer. I don't know whatever became of him. He used to live up in the Orchard here, but he just disappeared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Summerfield put it over on all of them, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yes. I guess he's really made a hole that time. Yes. They hired everybody they could get a hold of to hold moonshiners into town. Yeah, you bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You ever heard that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I've heard about Summerfield going after moonshiners, but never about that roundup. Oh, yeah. He was going to sell the moonshine for him. Yeah. And they whined him and dined him out there in that farming country, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know Pat Malone?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. I know Pat Malone. He was in Bovill.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That was his job was supposed to be rounded up moonshiners, too, although I don't think he ever did much of it. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I spoke to Hap Moody in Moscow.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You might know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he still living?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yes. In Moscow. Hell, he is. Well, he ain't no spring chicken. He's in his 90s, I'd say. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Well, Moody was with him when they rounded him up. I'm sure Moody was with him when they rounded up that outfit in the farm country. You asked him about that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He's told me a number of his escapades. Hap has, but didn't mention that particular one. Yeah. Listen, there was moonshine being made all around Elk River, too.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Not too much. There was a few.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I heard there was a black man that sold moonshine around Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him at all?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. A Negro. Oh, yes. He was a lumberjack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't he?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I don't know. I don't know. I just heard there was a... Yeah, I tell you. I know. I know that for a... He used to... He was a great canto man. But I think he... He left there for a while, and I think he moved... bootlegged, and he didn't moonshine. I think he just bootlegged. And

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I come along on a saddle hose one time, and I had a coat of whiskey in the saddlebag, and I just came by the roadway where this nigger was working, and I asked him if he wanted a drink. You know, I never seen whiskey go down in the bottle as fast as that. Boy, he just... Well, I'll let him have it. Yeah, that was right in Shadow's Meadow there, where they rode across the Shadow's Creek going into Elk River. That's where he had the roadway. He was heading the roadway. There was a kid down to him with a team from up above.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you happen to know whether he was one of the Welles' from Deary?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Because I know Chuck Wells worked in the woods, and he was black.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I don't. No, I don't. No. I wouldn't be a bit surprised if he was.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He had a reputation as being quite a hand in the woods.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Chuck Wells... Well, this guy was an awful good canto man. He was... Boy, he could just one make one of them things seen. Oh, yeah. But you know, one time,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: a man was held up, and the gun was just that far from me. This is where my hand is now. And I never made the move.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever heard of Billy Holmes, the camp foreman?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I was working for Billy Holmes, and my bed was right next to the door.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And this bootlegger came into camp. He had both cooked in camp, and he was an old penitentiary guy, this old bootlegger. And he was going to sell this suitcase full of... We called it white line, you know. It was alcohol. You diluted it with water, and you got whiskey. It was... He was pretty stout, you know. Well, Billy Holmes had taken his suitcase away from him. And he was coming through the car. With the suitcase. And right in front of me, the door opened up. And this guy stuck a gun right in Billy Holmes' stomach. And I said, give me that suitcase. Billy Holmes just... My soul gave him the suitcase. And the guy took the suitcase and held the gun, and then he... He guess he closed the door, so the door opened in. And he closed the door, and so that was just Billy Holmes. And I and Billy Holmes just turned to me and said, I'll tell you, George. Whenever anybody sticks a gun up, my guts is jacking up with me.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But that ain't the last of the story. They had a hog pen and a hog house in this camp, but they butchered all the hogs in the fall. So this hog house just stood empty. Oh, up from camp, probably a little further from here to that house. Towards Elk River, up the track from the camp. And we'd been to town a bunch of us when we'd stayed in town over Saturday night, and we were walking back Sunday evening, back to camp. And when we came close to camp and looked at the hog house, here was a couple of guys standing back with a hog house, just dancing and having a hell of a time. There's about 18 inches of snow on the ground, colder than hell. And we went over there, and here was the suitcase with all them 24 -quarters pints of white lining. Well, I'll tell you, we bused a drum camp that night. We just took the whole goddamn work into camp, and we just distributed it around. We never sold any of it. We just gave it away. Boy, I'll tell you, we had a time. We never saw that bullcuck again, though. Evidently, he just cashed the suitcase there and took off. The problem, maybe he was afraid that Billy Holmes was going to be after him. Yeah, I think so. I think so. Because it was the same suitcase.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What camp was this?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Camp B.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing in that camp? What was your job?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I was hooking on the landing. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far was that from the Elk River?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just fairly close, wasn't it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, after you leave the park, you go up towards Elk River, you leave where the park road takes off, and then the road bends to the left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you noticed there's a kind of a flat place there on the other side?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's where the camp was. Not right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the old kind of bunk houses they had there? Were they log bunk houses?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, they were on flat cars. Oh, they were on flat cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were those like to live in?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, they were all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, they were all right to live in. You bet. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that 1917 strike? The IWWs in 1917?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, see, I worked in the sawmill then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened in the sawmill?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, they didn't strike. No, they didn't strike. Sawmill didn't strike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mill never went down?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But a lot of guys had cards, though, who were working in the mill. That's what I've heard.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Maybe that's not true. Very few. Very few. For the fact, I don't know of anybody around the mill that had cards. No, I don't. No, I worked in the, I was millwright.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: In the training mill. And there was no, no strike at all. But I tell you, I went down to St. Mary's. I was going with the girl down, St. Mary's. Now, I took a few days off and went down there.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I got through with my visit, and I was going to go back to Elch River. So I came downtown with my traveling bag, and of course, I was all dressed up necktie, you know, and so on. I seen somebody that I knew when I got downtown, from Miltown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, where you going, Joe?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Joe, I said, I'm going up to Elch River. Oh, you can't get out of town. You can't get out of town. You got to go up to the Grand Theater. You know, there's a big house there. They used to call it the Grand Theater. And you got to go up there and get a permit to leave town. Anybody that looked like a lumberjack to put them in a stockade, you know, in St. Mary's. And Jesus Christ, you were getting close to where the train was going to leave. And I came up the Grand, and here was a line from here, clear across the street. Guys waiting to get permits to get out of town. Oh, I thought, hell, I thought, I'll never make it here. So I just walked down the depot. And here was a bunch of guys from both sides, you know, and I just took my traveling bag and just walked right straight along. And nobody said a word. I just walked. I didn't look like a lumberjack. They just passed me up. I looked more like a traveling salesman, I guess. Yes, right. With a necktie and overcoat and traveling bag, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That reminds me of a friend of mine back in the old country. During the Second World War, it was an export ban on quicksilver from Sweden. They had very little quicksilver and what they had, they needed for

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: blow ointment, if you know what that is. That's lard and quicksilver mix.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He was going up to this town on the border to Finland with a bunch of quicksilver. They were going to smuggle over. And the train was about 20 miles from this town. When the conductor came through and said, this train is going to stop and at the depot we are pulling down on the pier, there's going to be body inspection. God, I know he got to sweating. He didn't know what to do. And he was just loaded with quicksilver bottles.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Finally, when the train stopped on the pier, he just took his traveling bag and just walked through the line. He was a custom officer lined up on both sides to search people. Nobody woke up until after he got past the end of the line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One of the last guys said, hey, mister, wait a minute, where are you going?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, that's all right. I'll be a colleague with you tomorrow. Oh, okay, okay. He passed himself off as a custom officer.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That takes guts. That takes guts. He's got two brothers out in Seattle. This fellow has. This fellow never came over to this country. Yeah, that takes guts. I don't know what I would have done. I think I ditched the quicksilver.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, quicksilver and they call it Salva San. It's the 606.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they used to use for syphilis?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Them two things they were export band on. First, they didn't know when Sweden was going to get into it. They might need it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men in the camps, like around Elk River, what did they use for their license stuff?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I mean, there was a lot of bugs and graybacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they use that? What they used for that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Subutila and blue ornament. Subutila, you get that at the drugstore. And you just have to doctor yourself up and doctor your bed close up with Subutila. And you get rid of them. Oh, yeah. Oh, and those homestead cabins, they weren't lousy with them. Anytime you took a hunting trip down towards Big Island and stayed in a fuel log cabin, you came home lousy half of the time. Oh, yeah. Yeah, oh, God. That's the only time I had body life. I had to laugh. I had to laugh. I'd been out on a hunting trip and I got lousy. And I got some blue ornament and some Subutila and I went back to camp.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I was fixing myself up and here was a farmer by the name of Earl Hood. His older brother run the store there in Elk River. I don't know if you have or run across the name Hood. And Earl, he was quite a guy. He'd been through, oh, he'd been through high school and I guess part of college. And he'd been through some kind of a military academy and all. He came out to camp and he didn't have any bedding, but he got a job. Billy Holmes put him on. So could have worked the next morning. He came over and asked me if he'd be all right if he'd sleep with me. Sure. I said, you bet you can, I'll tell you. I'm lousy. I'm trying to get rid of him, but I'm lousy. But if you want to take a chance, you can sleep with me. Oh, that's all right. I'm lousy too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you sleep with him? Are you willing to sleep with him then?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Didn't much matter. You both lost. It didn't matter. I had my bed coat pretty well taken care of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that work?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: It would happen that oftentimes two lumberjacks would have shared the same bedding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That happened a lot, didn't it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. No. I thought it did. No. No, no. Everybody slept alone. Well, there would be a few South Europeans and so on that didn't sleep together, but main lumberjack. We never did that. Oh, I just let her sleep. I mean, of course, she didn't have any bedding. We were good friends anyway. We used to be out hunting together way out in those white rocks, springs and frees out and up into where we used to go. Her was a regular wood rat. He felt best when he was out in the woods with a gun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many lumberjacks have syphilis and have problems with that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Some. There was not quite a lot, but there was some gonorrhea, but not too much syphilis that I know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: To actually know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Only knew one guy that he was supposed to have syphilis. And of course, I might have been all wrong in this case. There was a young fellow there in our bunkhouse and there was this syphilitic guy too, and he was taken off and he wanted to sell his bedding to this young fellow. And I told him, don't buy that goddamn bedding. He's got syphilis. This guy got madder than him, but I didn't give a shit because I didn't know if it could affect this guy or not. Maybe not. But of course, I didn't know too much about it then. I was very outspoken those days.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Nobody argued with me very much, if you understand what I mean. I was pretty independent. I wasn't used to be argued with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In that way, do you think you were different from most guys?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think most guys would just kind of be quiet and not say anything one way or another. I mean, you were outspoken. I'm wondering, it sounds like a little different than what I hear about most of them. I hear a lot of lumberjacks were kind of mean at times, but I mean, if they got riled.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, yeah. That was mean all the time.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, not all the time, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that - Was you spoke your mind?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You got damn right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That gets you in trouble?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah, sometime.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Always managed to get out of it. On my terms.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Just like a friend of mine from Seattle talked to my wife a couple, two years ago. In the old country, even the police were scared of him.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I got a grandson. In our town here. And here, two years ago, he went to the bank. He was 11 years old. He went to the bank and drew out some money and bought himself a motorcycle. Then he takes off down the street and the police got after him. He hit for hold. He just give her the gun and hit for hold. So Mark at Pinsport, driving without a license, driving with a vehicle that wasn't registered and eluding the police.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I was reminiscing with my wife and I said, well, I said, Mark is doing all right. I said, but of course, if he's gonna catch up with grandpa, he still got to get to the point where the police are eluding him. Don't you go and put anything like that in that boy's head.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I said, the police starts to move in and then he's catching up with grandpa. He was up for the judge here a week ago, too. He's 13 now. And he got himself another motorcycle. And I don't know what happened, but he came across the street and here, this other motorcycle come and are banged up his machine and he banged up the other guy's machine.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He was up for the judge. It cost his dad $70 for the other guy's machine. All of them happened to Mark. He got all strapped up and he got a band fender on his motorcycle, but he got out of it pretty lucky. He didn't have any half -finished stitches or anything. I never, I'm gonna see the judge. The judge used to work for me. Martin Huff, he worked for me once over when he was a young fella. And I didn't say as he ran, I see Martin. He goes to unity quite a bit, Martin. Sometimes we go to unity. Next time I see him, I'm gonna ask him how my grandson performed in court. He was up for Martin. Martin didn't know it was my grandson.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I heard about lumberjack fights.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There was very little fighting like that, you know, like in the back east. No, that wasn't here. You know, they used to have Bull of the Woods back east and fight, you know, and court, shoe him and all that. No, no. Not here. No, we were too civilized for that here. No wonder what the difference was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, I think that was a lot amongst those French Canadians, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no, we were a pretty decent sort of a bunch. Awful independent, but pretty decent.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no. That's a rule.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: We were... We were good friends. The whole camp was friends. We were friendly the whole camp, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, you'd take Billy Holmes, Camp D, you know. Every one of us was friends.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I guess the old Charlie Red Jock in Elk River. I guess he used to be a kind of a Bull of the Woods back east. He was built like a gorilla.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, oh, oh. Bill Morris, the druggist, they get team mad once a day, regular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would? Huh? You would?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, Bill Morris, the druggist, would get Charlie Red Jock. Charlie Red Jock would go by the drugstore and go down to their post office and get his mail, and then he'd have to go in the drugstore and see Bill about something before he left the drugstore and be laddy mad. And he always come back for more. And one time,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: that was during the Depression, you know, for a while you could buy eggs down there for six cents a dozen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They were six cents for about a week, and then they went up to seven cents, and they stayed that way for quite a while, for a month or two, a seven cents a dozen. Well, Charlie Red Jock got him a car, and him and his wife went down there and bought a half a case of eggs for six cents a dozen. So when he came back from the post office, he had to go into the drugstore and tell Bill about it. After he got through terror and Bill says, I don't believe it. But, but, but, but, but, but, but, but, but, but, but, but the wife and I, we went down, and I, I bought him a sense, and I, I don't know. Bill says, I still don't believe it. Oh, Jesus. Charlie Red Jock was so mad, he was just hopping up and down. Finally, Bill said, now listen, Charlie. You know that debt don't pay for the wear and tear on the hen's asshole. And Charlie had got just torn out of there. He just tore out of that place. But he'd come back for more the next day. He'd have to see him about something, and he'd let him be mad before he'd leave. And he could be so sober about us. I don't believe that he'd say. Yeah, I guess Charlie used to be full of the world.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But you know, you're talking about, about you saying, you know, pretty much doing what you wanted to and being, and being, having people listen to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, you know, what you're saying, what kind of, what kind of thing would make you, situation would make you do that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I mean, just be outspoken as hell and do, you know, you know, lay it out your own way.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, if I think I'm right, I say it. I make a damn fool of politicians. You know, I'll say it.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'm just thinking of back in the, you know, in the woods and the, the sort of thing that you'd, you'd stand up for. I mean, what would be this, the kind of thing you'd, you'd have to, you know, really come out and say what you thought.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I'll just give you an example. You heard of, oh, all right.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: T .P. Jones. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I was working for T .P. Jones and Bill Morris up at the Placer Mine, up in Swamp Creek, you know, when it was putting in that steam shovel in there. So we had one fellow there by the name of,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I can't think of his name now. He was a steam shovel man and he was the awfulest yes man I've ever seen.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Baker was his name. He was from Bova. Anything, T .P. Jones, he was just old enough that he'd lost his good judgment. But anything that, T .P. Jones said, Baker say amen. Well, I could, if I could find anything wrong with it, that's a crazy old man.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, here came up, we had to make a rocker arm. And we had a pipe and we had a bar. And I said to T .P., we'll just flatten that pipe and stick that bar in it and drill a couple of three holes and put rivets through. And that would hold a bar. We had a drill press right there, one of them hand presses, you know. No, by God, George, we're going to weld it. So T .P. and I, we were all foreknown, trying to weld that thing with white fur bark. And I was either pulling that hand forward too fast or too slow. And we weren't no further along at noon than we were in the morning when we started. And he asked Baker, oh yeah, Baker thought it could be welded.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: So we went up and had learned Mrs. Jones cooked. She run the cook shack, Mrs. T .P. Jones. And then after we had ate, T .P. came in the men's tent where we gathered after we ate. T .P. fidgeted around and said, George, yeah, we're going to weld that thing this afternoon or I'm going to bust the gut. I just turned to the other man and I said, you know, the old man is going to bust the gut. This afternoon. And he looked at me. He was the big boss, you know. So we went out in the blacksmith's shop. And T .P. he fidgeted around and he stood there and fidgeted. George, we're going to rivet that goddamn thing.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Another time we had we had a bunch of those small rails that he was in lumber yards. You know, and we had a hack saw him off for the sluice boxes. They were going to use them for ripples in the sluice boxes. And you know what one of these blacksmith vices are. You know, they have a kind of a stand that comes down. Yeah, I think you've seen them. Well, Baker and T .P. was putting up the vise. And after they got it all put up, they had the vise this high. You know, and Bill Morris, the druggist, he was right there too, because I was his man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And after they got through and said, George, what do you think of that vise?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I said, it's too goddamn high.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the hell is the matter with you? Jesus Christ, who the hell is going to be able to use that vise?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Baker says it's all right. Okay. Okay. So, Baker and T .P. got the hacksaw in all pieces. T .P. Jones, he just barely made one piece, and Baker made another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he says, George, will you hacksaw one for us?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. I said, I won't hacksaw one or nothing for nothing for you. No, sir. You put up the vise and you hacksaw. And Bill Morris spoke up. So I had to take a machine all apart and take whatever needed to be turned down or planned down to the machine shop and get it all in tip -top shape and then put the machine together again. words: And a lot of times, those tables, you know. You see, there's cast iron tables in the planing machine. Well, everything runs against the guide. Here's the guide and everything. speaker: George Schmaltz
words: this end wears more than this end. You see, it's just a... a lot of averages. Of course, there's more, four and six. No matter what, if you're running twelve -inch, it's still wearing on this side. But if you're running ten -inch, there's two inches here that aren't wearing.

speaker: Sam Schrager

Of course, Bill Morris, the druggist, he went back to Japan on a trip. And he showed Bill Morris a lot of pictures that he took while he was there. And God, they were all uniform guys and high muck guys, you know. And Charlie Sagara was awful dressy. He didn't look... When he dressed up on a Sunday, he was really spic and span. Well, I said, tell you, Bird, I don't believe Neale has killed near as many deer as he says he has. Oh, yes, yes, he's killed a lot of them. Every man, woman and child, it was just like they'd, you would suck them between the eyes. We were waiting for it to start in the spring, and spring came and then all that work, the word comes, she's never starting again. Here we'd set the whole winter waiting for the mill to start. Bang. Just like that. There was a father there from Chicago. He was Bill Morris's brother -in -law. Frank Modine was his name. And oh, he wouldn't believe it. Oh no.
